original_thesis: The 'where to spend limited resources' stack. Trade-offs formalize what you're giving up; opportunity-cost quantifies the path not taken; prioritization orders the queue; feedback loops signal whether allocation is working; peer review catches blind spots in allocation logic; systems thinking reveals second-order effects of resource choices.
corrected_thesis: The 'spend where it actually matters' stack. Opportunity-cost quantifies the path not taken; prioritization orders the queue; decomposition breaks the decision into comparable options; critical thinking evaluates each option objectively; root-cause-analysis ensures you're allocating to the real problem, not a symptom; causal-attribution-resistance prevents misattributing where value comes from. This stack defeats peanut-butter spreading and allocation based on politics rather than impact.
what_this_stack_defeats: Peanut-butter spreading, ignoring opportunity cost, allocating to symptoms instead of causes
notes: Original thesis mentioned trade-offs, feedback-loops, peer-review, systems-thinking — not members. Actual members form a 'diagnostic allocation' stack: you diagnose the real problem first (causal forensics trunk) then allocate based on opportunity cost and prioritization. Prerequisite chain trade-offs → opportunity-cost is referenced but trade-offs isn't a member.
